children.name: Cinclus
children.description: Dippers — the only aquatic passerines. These stocky, dark-plumaged birds are perfectly adapted for life along fast-flowing streams. They walk and swim underwater, using their wings to 'fly' against the current while searching for insect larvae among the streambed stones. A transparent third eyelid (nictitating membrane) allows them to see underwater. Their loud, ringing song carries above the roar of rapids.

children.name: Cinclus cinclus
children.description: The European dipper, found across Europe, North Africa, and the Middle East east to Iran. A plump, dark bird with a brilliant white bib. Inhabits clean, fast-flowing streams and rivers. In Sweden it is resident in most regions, bobbing and dipping on streamside rocks. It can walk submerged for 30 seconds, foraging for mayfly and caddisfly larvae. Highly sensitive to water pollution, making it an important bioindicator.

children.name: Cinclus mexicanus
children.description: The only dipper in the Americas, ranging from Alaska south to Panama. Slate-grey plumage with a white eyelid flash. Found exclusively along clear, fast-flowing mountain streams. Like all dippers, it hunts underwater, walking along the streambed with its wings partly spread for stability.

children.name: Cinclus leucocephalus
children.description: Found in the Andes from Colombia to Bolivia. Distinctive white crown and throat contrasting with dark body. Inhabits high-altitude torrential streams between 1,000 and 4,000 metres. Forages underwater in the icy meltwater of Andean glaciers.

children.name: Cinclus pallasii
children.description: The largest dipper, found from the Himalayas east through China, Southeast Asia, and Taiwan to northeastern Russia and Japan. Uniformly dark chocolate-brown with no white bib. Inhabits mountain streams in dense forest. Its powerful song is audible above the loudest rapids.
